title: What The Most Successful People Do Before Breakfast

chapters.title: Reclaim the hours before sunrise
chapters.text:
Plenty of us watch mornings evaporate without meaningful progress. Laura Vanderkam names that helpless feeling: eyes on the clock, energy spent on low-value tasks, while genuinely important work gets pushed aside.

As a working mother, Vanderkam wrestles with the same tension — career demands on one side, family on the other — and the need to enter each day with the right mindset. Most of us mirror that struggle. Even after arriving at work, we burn prime hours on email triage or idle browsing. By mid-morning the best window has closed, along with much of our focus.

She recommends reshaping mornings around three priorities:

• Building career momentum through deliberate morning habits.
• Giving family relationships protected, unhurried time.
• Carving space for hobbies, meditation, exercise, or prayer.

Mornings need not be chaotic or wasted by default.

High performers treat early hours as strategic assets. Vanderkam points to figures like former PepsiCo CEO Steve Reinemund and leadership expert James Citrin, whose morning rituals supported outsized results.

Keep in mind that your morning rituals shouldn't be of the self-flagellation variety. Choose things you actually enjoy. ~ Laura Vanderkam

Many people launch routines they cannot sustain. Vanderkam argues that inconsistency is solvable — and that mornings are where willpower is richest.

chapters.title: Morning is when willpower peaks
chapters.text:
What separates average performers from exceptional ones? Average workers defer hard tasks until evening, then never reach them once fatigue sets in. Successful people front-load demanding work because morning willpower is difficult to recreate later in the day.

Willpower does not refill during the day; allocation is the only lever you control.

Gym-goers who schedule workouts after work often lose the battle against deadlines and demanding bosses. Serious exercisers tend to train before the day accelerates — a choice that demands discipline and a willingness to ignore morning chaos.

Roy F. Baumeister's research shows willpower erodes with exhaustion, much like muscle fatigue. Self-control weakens as the day wears on, which explains impulsive evening choices. Dieting is easier at breakfast; by nightfall we grant ourselves exceptions we would reject at dawn.

Like bodybuilders who accept discomfort to grow stronger, you can convert draining tasks into habits. Willpower bleeds across work, deep thinking, communication, and online distraction. The smart move is tackling the hardest items — workouts, tedious projects — while resolve is intact. Repetition then turns unpleasant chores automatic.

At that point, early-morning productivity requires less strain, preserving endurance for whatever comes next. Brushing teeth needs no extra willpower once habitual.

Once things become habitual, they operate as automatic processes, which consume less willpower. ~ Roy F. Baumeister, Ph.D

chapters.title: Prioritize what matters, not what screams
chapters.text:
Many mornings feel locked into a script: wake, groom, breakfast, commute. That script is not mandatory. Reorder the start of your day to invest in important work rather than merely urgent noise.

Have your job in the afternoon but keep your career in the morning.

Chores like laundry or lawn care feel pressing yet often run on autopilot thanks to modern convenience. They should not consume mental bandwidth better spent on growth.

James Citrin argues for dedicating mornings to critical pursuits. He exercises before 6 a.m. daily, joined by eighteen senior executives who rise equally early for high-leverage activities.

Important-but-not-urgent work — reading, writing, meditation — demands extra motivation because payoff is delayed. Morning willpower supplies that patience, making dawn ideal for long-horizon habits.

The most successful people use their mornings to develop any of these three things: their careers, relationships, or themselves.

Career growth requires tasks you cannot squeeze into a packed workday. Email can wait for office hours; strategic projects cannot. Research favors small daily writing sessions over occasional marathons. Consistency compounds.

Debbie Moysychyn, an executive who championed open-door collaboration, found constant meetings left no room for deep project work. She reclaimed mornings for focused execution, then used afternoons for culture-building without sacrificing her own initiatives.

chapters.title: Invest morning energy in relationships
chapters.text:
Relationships anchor a fulfilling life, yet evening exhaustion often steals the quality time we promise loved ones. Mornings offer a better currency: attention before depletion.

Whatever your household looks like, a strategy that mobilizes early hours is essential. Kathryn Beaumont Murphy, formerly of Saul Ewing Arnstein & Lehr LLP, discovered she had no time for her daughter after working late.

Family time is the input you must not negotiate at the expense of love.

Her law firm rewarded late hours, not early starts. Murphy flipped the pattern — earlier bedtimes, richer mornings with her daughter, who embraced the change. The whole household shifted toward a shared breakfast ritual. Remarkably, arriving later at the office did not dent her output under the firm's late-hours culture.

Her story invites creative morning design. Intimacy with a partner, often joked about, genuinely benefits from morning energy. Maintaining physical closeness supports healthy relationships. Later, organize a family breakfast while everyone is still home.

Career and family form two pillars of an accomplished life; personal development is the third deserving its share of morning time.

Self-care is the first casualty when responsibilities swell. Under deadline pressure we sacrifice rest and renewal.

Identify what renewal means for you — fitness, writing, reading, meditation, prayer — then claim it by rising earlier. The minutes exist; they simply need guarding.

Did you know? An average person aged 30-45 wakes up by 5:59 a.m., but they still don't start their official duties until 8:00 or 9:00 a.m.

chapters.title: Picture the morning you want
chapters.text:
We shape our routines — yet cling to habits that block progress. Vanderkam outlines five shifts; here are the first two.

Keep records of your time expenditure

Nights matter as much as mornings. Pre-bed behavior determines how you wake. Tracking evening time sinks reveals energy thieves disguised as necessities. Scrubbing the house at 10:30 p.m. feels justified when days are packed, yet dishes reappear by morning and spotless windows rarely change your life. Reserve deep cleaning for weekends, as you would a favorite show. Protect sleep first.

Wasting dawn on low-value tasks breeds frustration. Older children can dress and feed themselves; spouses can manage their own routines. Answering email at 7 a.m. rarely matters when no one expects a reply. Logging idle hours builds awareness of the 168 hours in each week.

Visualize your ideal morning

Ideal mornings differ by interests, obligations, and relationship status. Mental rehearsal sets a productive rhythm for the day. Bake in activities that energize you — yoga, painting, reading, gardening.

Picturing our perfect morning brings excitement in advance. Living it out is using that fire to progress.

Vanderkam runs unless romance wins the alarm clock, shares breakfast with family, then writes — books and blog posts — before the workday claims her. Find the balance between needs and aspirations, then build a routine around it.

chapters.title: Turn vision into a workable plan
chapters.text:
You have tracked a week and imagined a stronger morning. Now test whether the picture can survive real life.

Answer two logistical questions:

• How much time does the ideal routine require?
• What concrete changes make it possible?

Morning cooking may demand earlier wake-ups and earlier bedtimes.

A journey without a plan is prone to calamities.

Focus on making your morning rituals easier

A clear vision still needs implementation. Map necessary activities against the clock so mornings stay aligned with the rest of the day. A graphic time division of necessary activities allows us to keep our morning in touch with the rest of the day.

Develop the habit

Adopt one habit at a time to channel morning energy productively. Track progress because routines take weeks to stick. Enjoyable habits are easier to keep; delightful mornings raise motivation to leave bed. Reward successful adoption — a favorite meal, new clothes, or another treat you genuinely enjoy.

Tune Up as necessary

Do not fossilize rituals. New jobs, cities, or life stages warrant adjustment. The goal is optimal use of mornings, not rigid adherence.

Life surprises. It is tempting to discard an ideal routine after disruption, so build backup plans for backup plans.

Vanderkam paused running during her first pregnancy, redirecting that time toward other self-care. After birth she returned to forty-five-minute runs. Expect periodic recalibration and make room for it.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Many assume sleeping later equals more productivity — more rest, more strength. Rest matters, but the logic is backwards. Go to bed early and rise early instead.

Sometimes, when asked what we did during the week, we can answer one thing: work. We do much more than that, yet countless hours slip past unnoticed.

Morning is peak productivity. Reclaim awareness from dawn onward while creativity is highest. Changing habits is hard; the payoff is worth it.

Dedicate early hours to professional growth, personal development, and relationships — not to the evening, when exhaustion leaves you numb. Experimenting with a new routine is itself mental training.

Consider meditation, exercise, family breakfast, or industry reading before the inbox opens. Willpower outlasts evening resolve, making the wee hours the best window to improve your life.

Try this

• For one week, log hourly activities — rough estimates are fine.
• Sort entries into important and unimportant.
• Trim unimportant time to fund what matters.

Read it till the end?
